elements.sent-2411-001: This paper investigates methods for calculating the chromatic symmetric function (CSF) of a graph in chromatic-bases and the $m_\lambda$-basis.
elements.sent-2411-002: Our key contributions include a novel approach for calculating the CSF in chromatic-bases constructed from forests and an efficient method for determining the CSF in the $m_\lambda$-basis.
elements.sent-2411-003: As applications, we present combinatorial proofs for two known theorems that were originally established using algebraic techniques.
elements.sent-2411-004: Additionally, we demonstrate that an algorithm introduced by Gonzalez, Orellana, and Tomba arises as a special case of our proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-005: In the year 1995, Stanley introduced a symmetric function for a simple graph $ G $ as described below:.
elements.sent-2411-006: The chromatic symmetric function (CSF) of $G$, denoted by $ X_G(x) $, is defined as follows: \begin{equation}X_G(x) \triangleq \sum_{\kappa} \prod_{v \in V(G)} x_{\kappa(v)},\end{equation} where the sum ranges over all proper colorings $\kappa : V(G) \to \mathbb{Z ^{+}$.}
elements.sent-2411-007: A proper coloring $\kappa$ assigns a color (a positive integer) to each vertex such that no two adjacent vertices share the same color.
elements.sent-2411-008: By setting $ x_i = 1 $ for $ 1 \leq i \leq k $ and $ x_j = 0 $ for $ j > k $, we obtain $ \chi_G(k) $, the one-variable chromatic polynomial that counts the number of proper colorings of $ G $ with $ k $ colors.
elements.sent-2411-009: In his seminal paper, Stanley expressed $ X_G $ using classical bases of symmetric functions, proved many results, and proposed several conjectures related to $ X_G $.
elements.sent-2411-010: This graph-based symmetric function has attracted significant interest since; see, for example, \cite{martin2008distinguishing, orellana2014graphs, cho2015chromatic, aliste2017trees, aliste2023marked, chan2024graph, sagan2024chromatic .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Most research related to $ X_G $ revolves around two main conjectures: the $e$-Positivity Conjecture~\cite{stanley1995symmetric , which states that if a poset is $(3+1)$-free, then its incomparability graph is a nonnegative linear combination of elementary symmetric functions; and the Tree Isomorphism Conjecture, which posits that the CSF distinguishes non-isomorphic trees.}
elements.sent-2411-012: The first conjecture was proved recently by Hikita~\cite{hikita2024proof , but the later one is only known to hold for trees with fewer than 30 vertices~\cite{heil2018algorithm and has been verified for certain subclasses of trees~\cite{martin2008distinguishing, loebl2019isomorphism .}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Another area of focus has been finding families of graphs for which $ X_G $ is Schur positive, primarily due to connections to the representation theory of the general linear and symmetric groups.
elements.sent-2411-014: Another line of research investigates generalized versions of the CSF, which contain $ X_G $ as a specialization, for example, the $ q $-quasisymmetric function of Shareshian and Wachs~\cite{shareshian2016chromatic , non-commutative versions~\cite{gebhard2001chromatic , a rooted version~\cite{loehr2024rooted , and weighted versions~\cite{crew2020deletion .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Recent investigations into $ X_G $ have tried using a linear combination relation.
elements.sent-2411-016: In a recent paper, Alieste-Prieto, De-Mier, Zamora, and a second author~\cite{aliste2023marked introduced the Deletion-Near-Contraction relation (DNC relation).}

elements.sent-2411-437: Returning to the reconstruction conjecture, an intriguing question arises: is it possible to reconstruct a tree’s deck of cards using $\lambda$-matrices? elements.sent-2411-439: The number of forests and trees with $n$ vertices far exceeds $p(n)$, the dimensions of the corresponding spaces.

elements.sent-2411-444: The chromatic symmetric function (CSF) for a simple graph was first introduced by Stanley in 1995 as follows:.
